FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data at Year 2 assessment (2018-2020), N=9,008 early adolescents

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures problematic social media use via Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ) with six items assessing mood modification, salience, relapse, conflict, and tolerance

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models testing association between problematic social media use and alcohol expectancies (positive and negative outcomes). Model 3: problematic SM use β=0.045 (p<0.001) for positive expectancies and β=0.072 (p<0.001) for negative expectancies

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients reported: B=0.045 (95% CI 0.020-0.069, p<0.001) for positive alcohol expectancies; B=0.072 (95% CI 0.043-0.101, p<0.001) for negative alcohol expectancies

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using cross-sectional data with adjusted linear regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in BMC Public Health, 2023, DOI:10.1186/s12889-023-15298-3; not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports cross-sectional analyses with smartphone/SM as predictor, not dependent variable. Even though analyses are cross-sectional, SM is independent variable predicting alcohol expectancies outcome (not SM outcome). Does not meet all three FT-EC7 conditions.
notes: Time spent on social media was not associated with expectancies, but problematic SM use showed strong associations. Sample included self-reported measures from ABCD Year 2 assessment.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data prospectively from Baseline and Year 2 (2018-2020), N=9,673 early adolescents (screen users)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures three smartphone/screen modalities: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), and Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ). All are specific phone/SM variables assessing problematic use.

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized logistic mixed effects models and generalized linear mixed effects models testing prospective associations between ACE score (demographic/contextual predictor) and problematic video game use, social media use, and mobile phone use. Multiple tests reported with ORs and continuous scores.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple ORs reported in Table 2: ACEs ≥4 associated with 3.1 times higher odds (2.05-4.73) of problematic video game use and 1.57 times higher odds (1.22-2.03) of problematic mobile phone use

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using prospective design with logistic and linear mixed effects models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in BMC Public Health, 2023, DOI:10.1186/s12889-023-16111-x; not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: All analyses have problematic screen use as the DEPENDENT variable (binary outcomes: problematic video game/SM/phone use). Predictors are ONLY demographic/contextual (ACE scores, age, sex, race/ethnicity, income, parent education, mental health covariates for confounding). Design is PROSPECTIVE/LONGITUDINAL (Baseline ACEs predicting Year 2 problematic screen use). All three FT-EC7 conditions are met.
notes: Prospective longitudinal design with demographic/contextual predictors only for screen use outcomes. Meets FT-EC7 exclusion criteria. Study notes low rates of problematic social media use (3.5%) possibly due to young age (11-12 years).

FT_IC1.evidence: Introduction to special issue of Health Psychology featuring ABCD Study research. References multiple papers analyzing ABCD data from baseline, Years 1 and 2 assessments.

FT_IC2.evidence: Special issue introduction/editorial. Does not present original specific SM/phone variable measurement. Describes papers addressing screen time and other outcomes but does not itself measure specific SM/phone variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Special issue introduction without original data analysis. No statistical tests or associations reported in this introduction itself.

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric coefficients, ORs, or p-values reported in introduction

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a special issue introduction/editorial/commentary. Describes other papers' work but contains no original empirical analysis or data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Health Psychology, 2023, Vol.42, No.12, DOI:10.1037/hea0001351

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply because FT_IC5 is NOT_MET. This is editorial/introduction with no original analysis. Per E10, special-issue intros without original analysis fail FT-IC5.
notes: This is a special issue editorial introduction describing a collection of papers on ABCD and adolescent health. It references papers on screen time effects but does not conduct original analysis itself. Fails on FT_IC5 (not original empirical analysis).
